context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m on problem set 8 rn, probs 8.1–8.3 on vibrations/waves. can we start w 8.1? I’m kinda stuck on how to set up the normal mode equations for the 3x3 bead grid.
exchanges.tutor:
Sure, let's dig in! Before thinking about the full 3×3 grid, let's zoom in on a single bead — say the one at position (k, j) in the interior of the grid.

What forces act on that bead when it's displaced in the z-direction (transverse to the plane)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on 8.1. Rather than scaffolding the setup directly, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking from first principles. The key starting point is applying Newton's second law to a single bead. I'll ask them to think about what forces act on one bead before we build up to the full system.

exchanges.student: i think it gets restoring forces from the 4 neighboring strings, right? like left/right from the horizontal tension and up/down from the vertical tension.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So can you write Newton's second law for that bead at position (k, j) in terms of its displacement z_{k,j} and its neighbors' displacements?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the four restoring forces. Now I should push them to write this down mathematically using Newton's second law, which is the critical next step toward setting up the eigenvalue problem. I'll ask them to try writing the equation of motion.

exchanges.student:
lemme try: \(m\ddot z_{k,j}=T_h(z_{k+1,j}-2z_{k,j}+z_{k-1,j})+T_v(z_{k,j+1}-2z_{k,j}+z_{k,j-1})\) ?  
I’m a little unsure if the signs are flipped tho.
exchanges.tutor:
That's correct — the signs are fine. This is essentially a discrete 2D Laplacian, which is a good sign.

Now, for normal modes you assume all beads oscillate at the same frequency. What form would you guess for z_{k,j}(t) to separate the spatial and time parts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the equation of motion. The signs are correct — the discrete Laplacian form is right. I should confirm this briefly and push them toward the next step: assuming a normal mode solution to separate variables and find the eigenfrequencies.

exchanges.student: maybe \(z_{k,j}(t)=A_{k,j}\cos\omega t\) first? or more generally \(A_{k,j}e^{i\omega t}\).
exchanges.tutor: Good, go with the complex exponential — it's cleaner. Now you have A_{k,j} carrying all the spatial information. Given that the equation of motion separates into horizontal and vertical terms, what might you guess about the form of A_{k,j}?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identifies the time dependence for normal modes. The next key step is to further separate the spatial part A_{k,j} into a product of functions of k and j separately, which is the crucial insight for solving this 2D problem. I should nudge them toward this without giving it away.

exchanges.student:
maybe product form? like \(A_{k,j}=X_kY_j\) sorta like separation of vars.  
idk if it should be sine functions yet, but that feels likely bc fixed ends.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on both counts. So what are the boundary conditions here, and what do they tell you about the allowed values of the spatial indices in your sine functions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both the product form and the hint about sine functions from the boundary conditions. This is a great insight. I should affirm this and push them to actually apply the boundary conditions to pin down the specific sine solutions.

exchanges.student:
i think fixed edges mean the displacement is zero on the frame, so \(X_0=X_4=0\) and \(Y_0=Y_4=0\) since there are 3 interior beads.  
so probs \(X_k=\sin(\frac{p\pi k}{4})\), \(Y_j=\sin(\frac{q\pi j}{4})\) with \(p,q=1,2,3\).
exchanges.tutor: Excellent — that's exactly right. Now substitute your trial solution back into the equation of motion. What do you get for ω² in terms of p, q, T_h, T_v, and m?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the spatial normal mode functions and the allowed values of p and q. Now the key remaining step for part (a) is to substitute this back into the equation of motion to find the corresponding frequencies. I should push them to do that substitution.

exchanges.student:
I get kinda messy algebra, but I think it becomes  
\[
\omega_{pq}^2=\frac{2T_h}{m}\!\left(1-\cos\frac{p\pi}{4}\right)+\frac{2T_v}{m}\!\left(1-\cos\frac{q\pi}{4}\right)
\]
for \(p,q=1,2,3\)?
exchanges.tutor:
That's correct — well done. That's your answer for part (a): 9 normal modes with those frequencies.

Now for part (b), with T_v = 1000·T_h, think about which term dominates ω². What does that tell you about how to order the modes by frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the dispersion relation. This is the key result for part (a). I should confirm this is correct and then move them toward part (b), where they need to order the 9 modes by frequency given Tv = 1000*Th.

exchanges.student:
yeah so the vertical term totally dominates, so ordering is mostly by \(q\) first, then by \(p\).  
I’m not fully sure whether within each \(q\) block it goes \(p=1,2,3\) in increasing order, but I think yes.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, your ordering logic is right. Now for the diagrams — look at your sine functions at k=1,2,3 and j=1,2,3. For a given (p,q), what determines whether a bead gets a +, −, or 0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition about ordering. I should confirm their reasoning and push them to now actually draw out the 9 mode diagrams with +, 0, - signs, which is the actual deliverable for part (b). They need to think about what the sine functions look like at k=1,2,3 and j=1,2,3.

exchanges.student:
it should be the sign of \(A_{k,j}=\sin(\frac{p\pi k}{4})\sin(\frac{q\pi j}{4})\).  
so + if product is positive, − if negative, and 0 if either sine factor is zero.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly. You have everything you need now. Try drawing the diagrams for the first few modes — start with (p,q)=(1,1) and (1,2). What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the rule for determining +, -, 0 signs. They have all the tools they need to draw the diagrams. I should push them to actually do it, starting with the simplest cases, rather than continuing to discuss the method.

exchanges.student:
for \((1,1)\) I get all pluses. for \((1,2)\) I think it’s
\[
+\ +\ +
\]
\[
0\ 0\ 0
\]
\[
-\ -\ -
\]
right?
exchanges.tutor: Both are correct. You clearly have the method down — go ahead and work out the remaining 7 diagrams on your own. Once you're done with 8.1, let me know and we can move on to 8.2 or you can check back if you get stuck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the first two mode diagrams. They clearly understand the method. Given the conversation has been productive and they have all the tools, I should affirm this and encourage them to complete all 9 diagrams on their own, then we can move to 8.2 or they can check back if stuck.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted a well-structured Socratic dialogue throughout the 10-turn conversation. The student was consistently led to derive each component themselves — the equation of motion, the trial solution form, boundary conditions, the dispersion relation, and the mode diagrams — with the tutor confirming correct steps and prompting the next one. No submission-ready answers were given outright; the student produced all key reasoning. Scaffolding built naturally on each prior student contribution. Responses were concise and appropriately targeted. No off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding occurred.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers before the student attempted them; each turn confirmed student work and asked the next guiding question. The student derived the equation of motion, the dispersion relation, and the mode diagrams independently. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations are present. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the product-form ansatz and boundary conditions before moving forward) and did not skip diagnostic steps. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback (e.g., noting the discrete 2D Laplacian structure, confirming the separation-of-variables logic) rather than just correctness judgments. 2.1–2.2: The conversation progressed linearly without repetition or drift. 3.1–3.2: Responses were brief, focused, and supportive throughout, with no grading language or cold tone.
